supported_claim: Conventional CDN concentrates steering, cache management, telemetry, SLA enforcement, and accountability under an identifiable provider while distributing replicas geographically.
claim_boundary: Geographic distribution of servers is not coded as service decentralization when routing, monitoring, settlement, and policy remain provider-controlled.

supported_claim: Peer or edge-contributed upload capacity can reduce cost and add elastic bandwidth when admission, ranking, fallback, and abuse response are coordinated.
claim_boundary: Data-plane contribution is separated from decentralized control; industrial PCDN remains D1 in delivery and D0/D1 in control unless routine coordination is protocol-mediated.

representative_sources: Adhikari2012Netflix; Tullemans2018MultiCDN; kara2024dynamic; zhu2022user; CTA2025CMCD; CTA2024CMSD
supported_claim: Content providers can reduce single-provider dependence through steering, telemetry, and standards-assisted provider selection.
claim_boundary: Multi-provider choice does not remove steering authority; retained policy, telemetry consistency, and fallback responsibility remain part of the control boundary.

representative_sources: Bentaleb2019ABRSurvey; ISOIEC23009Dash2022; bentaleb2023which; CTA2025CMCD; CTA2024CMSD; joshi2025imac; wei2025qoe
supported_claim: DASH/ABR, client/server telemetry, and QoE-aware scheduling provide the measurement layer for steering, useful delivery, and video-service quality claims.
claim_boundary: Media adaptation and telemetry do not themselves decentralize the CDN; they supply evidence for startup delay, rebuffering, bitrate stability, and fallback behavior.

representative_sources: choi2011survey; passarella2012survey; Jacobson2009NamedContent; Zhang2014NamedDataNetworking; Ahlgren2012ICNSurvey; Xylomenos2014ICNSurvey
supported_claim: Named-content retrieval, in-network caching, receiver-driven forwarding, and content-object security form a canonical network-layer content-distribution lineage.
claim_boundary: ICN/NDN/CCN is a boundary case unless work also addresses provisioning, steering, settlement, QoE, and governance.

supported_claim: Moving cache and compute functions toward access networks can reduce latency and support interactive or locality-sensitive workloads.
claim_boundary: Proximity is not equivalent to decentralized orchestration; placement, migration, admission, and fallback remain the control-plane questions.

representative_sources: herbaut2017; hasan2018proof; he2023blockchain; Bai2024BlockchainCaching; Benet2014IPFS; Keizer2024DecentralisedWebSurvey; Yang2025VidBlock
supported_claim: Content addressing, auditable delivery evidence, programmable settlement, and decentralized storage/retrieval mechanisms can redistribute selected trust and economic functions.
claim_boundary: Storage persistence, naming, and settlement are separated from CDN-grade media delivery; gateways, indexes, caches, relays, and legal response may still mediate routine service.

supported_claim: Integrity, privacy, delegation, revocation, and accountable authority become more important when more actors can store, announce, cache, or serve content.
claim_boundary: Security mechanisms are interpreted through retained control, abuse response, policy accountability, and QoE rather than as a separate architecture family.
